turns.content: hey so i got a question for you do you know any haitian creole because i want to test you
I know some! Haitian Creole is a really interesting language — it's got French roots but with its own grammar and influences from West African languages. Go ahead, test me.
okay what does sa k pase mean because i say it all the time and i want to see if you actually know or if youre just going to guess
That one I definitely know — "sa k pase" means "what's up" or "what's happening." And the typical response is "n ap boule" — we're hanging in there, we're good. Did I pass?
okay yeah you got that one thats the easy one everybody knows that one even the non haitian kids in miami knew that one growing up lets try something harder what about mwen grangou
"Mwen grangou" — I'm hungry. "Mwen" is I/me and "grangou" is hungry. Are you telling me something right now or is this still the quiz?

ha both honestly im at a rest stop in maryland and the food options are terrible right now its either subway or a gas station hot dog that has been spinning since this morning but okay you got that one too let me give you a real one what about pa pale anpil

Ha, definitely skip the spinning hot dog. Okay — "pa pale anpil" — I believe that means "don't talk too much" or "stop talking so much." Like telling someone to keep it brief or stop running their mouth. How'd I do?

yeah thats pretty close my grandmother used to say that to me all the time when i was little because i never stopped talking and honestly nothing has changed i still dont stop talking i just do it into a phone now instead of at people in the room okay here is one that i think you will not get what does dechouke mean

Ooh, that's a deeper cut. "Dechouke" — if I'm remembering right, that has political meaning. It refers to uprooting or overthrowing, like during political upheaval in Haiti. It was used a lot during the fall of the Duvalier regime in 1986 — the people "dechouke" the government, pulled it out by the roots. Am I close?

okay im actually impressed right now because yeah thats exactly it my dad used to use that word when he talked about the old days in haiti before we left and its one of those words that carries so much weight you know like its not just a word its a whole history in one sound and i was listening to this podcast about haitian history the other day and they used the word but the host pronounced it wrong and it was driving me crazy the whole episode

That's the thing about Creole — the pronunciation carries meaning that you can't just read off a page. Someone who grew up hearing it knows immediately when it sounds wrong. Which podcast was it? Some of the history ones are great on content but rough on the pronunciation.

it was called revolutions revisited or something like that and the content was actually good like they were talking about toussaint louverture and the revolution and all that but every time they said a creole word it was like they were reading french and just guessing and thats not how it works creole is its own thing you know like people think its broken french and that drives me insane its a real language with real rules

That's a really common and frustrating misconception. Haitian Creole has its own syntax, its own grammar rules, its own way of handling tense and possession — it's not "simplified French" any more than English is "simplified German." They share vocabulary but the structure is completely different. The "broken French" thing is honestly kind of disrespectful to a language that millions of people speak as their primary tongue.

exactly thank you and its funny because when i was a kid in miami i used to be embarrassed to speak creole at school because the other kids would make fun of it and now i wish i spoke it more you know like i think in english mostly now and sometimes i have to search for the creole word and that makes me sad because its like im losing a piece of where i come from one more for you what does bondye bon mean

"Bondye bon" — God is good. "Bondye" comes from the French "bon Dieu" but in Creole it just means God, and "bon" is good. It's one of those phrases that people say as an expression of gratitude or faith, like acknowledging that things are working out.

And what you said about losing the Creole words — that's really common for people who immigrated young. But the fact that you still use phrases naturally means it's in there. You might just need more opportunities to use it.

yeah my mom still speaks it to me on the phone and i understand everything she says but i answer in english half the time and she gets on me about it she says jerome ou ap bliye lang ou which means youre forgetting your language and shes right i need to teach naomi some too before its too late you know a language dies when you stop passing it down okay im going to get the subway i guess spinning hot dog is not the move tonight mesi anpil for the language lesson in reverse ha

Ha, mesi anpil to you too — you taught me more than I taught you tonight! And teaching Naomi some Creole is a great idea. Even a few phrases she can say with her grandmother would mean the world. Enjoy the Subway, skip the death dog, and safe driving out there.
